State of California

Memorandum

To : To File                                     Date : Oct. 15, 1976

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ted Prim

Subject: Telephone advice provided to Mr. Kludt, City manager
         of San Gabriel, in response to questions posed by
         Mr. Richie, City Attorney

         Mr. Kludt informed me that the Planning Commission
had not adopted a Conflict of Interest Code. Therefore, I
told him that planning commissioners need not disclose gifts
at this time. However, if pursuant to a code or to Article
2 a city official were required to disclose gifts from the
Pacific Drive-In Theater, the following standards were applicable.

         The season pass is a gift and it undoubtedly is
worth more than $25, accordingly, it must be reported. To
determine its actual value the official should consult with
the donor. If the donor cannot place a value on the season
pass, the official may place an asterisk on the form in lieu
of a value, and then elsewhere explain why the gift has no
ascertainable value. I also told him that if the officer
chooses not to follow this procedure, he could report the
value as his good faith estimate.

         I also told him that no reporting obligations
whatsoever would attach if an official returned the season
pass unused within 30 days of receipt. Mr. Kludt promised
to forward this information to Mr. Richie.


TP:plh


FEDS TRANSMITTAL OF CORRESPONDENCE

CORRESPONDENCE__RITCHIE, Graham, San Gabriel City Atty.

DATE____10/5_________ RE:Questions re reporting of gifts

COPY TO:

ADMINISTRATION          CONFLICTS              ENFORCEMENT
_ROS T.                 _DCL                   _BOS S.
_LINDA                  _EP                    _RICA
_JUDY                   _KEN G.                _KNEERIN
_                       _AL                    _FRED

EXECUTIVE               LEGAL                  TADS
_DEP                    _X KEN F.              _JOHN M.
_MICKEY                 _X ARNAUD              _MAY
_CARDA                  _X_ITED               _GINA
                        _NITRO                 _MARYANN
                                               _JOHN G.
                                               _FAX
                                               _RICH

KEN F.______, Please:

Take whatever action you consider appropriate.

_X_ Reply direct by__10/15____; cc: Jeanne____________

___ Prepare suggested reply and return by_______________

___ Coordinate reply with___________________


7/24/53


FPPFC
Oct 5 9:29AM'76

CITY OF SAN GABRIEL
FOUNDED 1771
INCORPORATED 1913

532 WEST MISSION DRIVE • SAN GABRIEL, CALIFORNIA 91776 • (213) 282-4104 • 283-2591
P.O. BOX 130 • SAN GABRIEL,CALIFORNIA 91778

GRAHAM A. RITCHIE
CITY ATTORNEY

CITY WITH A MISSION

September 30, 1976

Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
1100 K Street Bldg.
Sacramento, California 95804

Gentlemen:

I have been requested by a member of the San Gabriel Planning Commission,
Mr. Tom Berge, to submit the following question regarding the reporting
of gifts under the Political Reform Act of 1974:

If a City official receives a courtesy pass from Pacific
Drive-In Theatres, which is nontransferable and nonsaleable,
entitling the recipient to attend motion pictures shown at
Pacific Drive-In Theatres, is such a gift reportable?

In that connection, it appears that the pass has no market
value. Would it make any difference whether the pass was
used during the year sufficiently often to equal $25 worth
of admissions? By what formula should the commissioner
value this gift in order to determine whether it is worth
$25 or not?

Enclosed for your further confusion is a copy of a recent newspaper
article indicating the obvious difficulty in placing a value on this
type of item. I would appreciate it if you would furnish Mr. Berge
with an informal opinion on this subject to guide his further reporting.

Yours very truly,

Graham A. Ritchie
Graham A. Ritchie
City Attorney

GAR:zz
Attach.


Aired gifts go to city leaders

By STEVE HEMMERICK
Staff Writer

Free passes to movie theatres and five-in, golf courses, football ames and horse races were among ts given to San-Jose area ity officials, according to their lorning interest statements on the local city halls.

Under the Political Reform Act of 4, city council members, mayors d city managers must report all ts of $25 or more if they have been eceived or have served for more han one year.

About a dozen city officials in 13 -ties received gifts amounting t least $500, and in some cases it s more than twice that amount.

President City Manager Donald F. Infyre reported that he received in om Bowl and Rose Parade

from the National Football League mission tickets from the Los Angeles Turf Club in Arcadia, and passes valued at $250 from Parthe-Five-in and Edwards theatres during the last ear.

An El Monte councilman, Thomas eiser, reported receiving $705 in ifts between March, 1975, and arch 1978. The gifts included a $100 heatre pass from Edwards Theatre, two $150 tickets to a dinner for Former Los Angeles County District Atty. Joe Busch, three $250 pass to the two $50 tickets to a dinner from Assemblyman Jose Morales, D- l Monte, and a $25 admission to a reception for Rep. George Danielson, D-Monterey Park.

In Monrovia, Councilman Robert T. Bartle said he received gifts passes from the Los Angeles Turf Club on the

Club and valued them at $500.
Monterey Park City Councilman Matthew G. Martinez said he received free passes worth $675. If it's included a pass to the Montere Park Golf Course, valued at $175, a pass to the Edwards Theatre valued at $300.

In South Pasadena, City Coun Pilgrim Fletcher II Swan reported receiving $450 in contributions from a campaign from April, 1975 to March, 1976.

While many city officials received less than $300 in gifts, some made all, some others put differing values on what gifts they did receive.

For example, Temple City City Manager Karl Koski said he received, between December, 1974, and October, 1975, passes from the Los Angeles Turf Club on the

Leaders get gifts

Continued from Page A1 tickets were valued at $500 by White, $700 by Mellenez and $300 by Wilfong.

City Directors Mortimer J. Matthews said he received a gift worth $4,500 in 31 shares of Procter and Gamble stock, which he received from Cecelia P. Matthews between May, 1975, and May, 1976.

In Arcadia, City Councilman Floretta R. Lauder said she received $350 worth of free passes, $150 in ad-

saturaay showtimes

Pacific Drive-Ins and Edwards theatres.

He said the passes from each organization but were worth more than $3, but he did not list their exact value.

In examining more than 75 sets of economic interest statements, the Star-News found that many were filled out inadequately or incorrectly. In some instances the wrong form was used.

According to the Fair Political Practices Commission (FPPC), so much confusion has arisen over filling out the forms that four people work in a Sacramento office just to answer questions and check statements concerning the forms.

The FPPC is a state agency which was set up in 1974 to oversee the provisions of the Political Reform Act.

The most common error was not filling out the income portion of the claim, exposes a confidential relationship with a client.

One court case has already been held on this matter and the trial judge upheld the code. The decision is expected to be appealed, but in the meantime the code stands.

Serving on an area city council generally brings the council member an income of about $2,000 annually although council members get paid nothing in two area cities.

Copies of statements obtained by the Star-News showed that all but two of the area officials required to file their income categories earned more than $10,000.

statement, according to an FPPC spokesman, who added that some of those required to file under the act think that only income made within their jurisdiction need be reported. However, the statement instruction manual states that all income must be disclosed, except for city salaries or reimbursements, inheritance and certain dividends and interest.

Many of the city clerks contacted by the Star-News agreed that the forms as well as the information sent by the FPPC are confusing.

City council members concurred and many added that they feel that statements are an invasion of their privacy.

"It's like presuming you're guilty," one council member said.

Some lawyers who sit on city councils protested that they have to list clients who pay them more than $1,000 in fees. That, they contend, exposes a confiden-

Workshop due in Altadena

An experimental workshop in assertive training will be conducted by the Altadena Guidance and Counseling Services, 1045 E. Mariposa Ave., Altadena, from 10 a.m. to 6 p.m. today.
